ideas.title: Create a Water Cycle in a Bag
steps: Pour about 1/4 cup of water into your plastic bag
Add 2-3 drops of blue food coloring to make the water easy to see
Seal the bag tightly so no water can escape
Use the marker to draw a sun in the top corner and some ground at the bottom
Tape your bag to a sunny window
Watch your bag over the next few hours and days
You will see water droplets form at the top of the bag
The droplets will slide down like rain
Draw what you observe and label evaporation, condensation, and precipitation
ideas.time: 20 minutes setup, then observe over 2-3 days
ideas.why_it_works: The sun heats the water and turns it into water vapor (evaporation). The vapor rises and cools at the top of the bag, forming tiny droplets (condensation). When the droplets get heavy enough, they fall back down (precipitation). This is exactly what happens in nature's water cycle, but you can watch it happen in just one bag!

ideas.title: Disappearing Water Experiment
steps: Fill both cups with exactly 1/2 cup of water
Use your marker to mark the water level on each cup
Place one cup on a sunny windowsill
Place the other cup inside a closed cupboard or drawer
Write down the date and time
Check both cups at the same time each day for 5 days
Mark the new water levels with your marker and write the date
Measure how much water is left in each cup
Compare which cup lost more water and why
ideas.time: 15 minutes setup, 5 minutes daily for 5 days
ideas.why_it_works: Water evaporates into the air as invisible water vapor. The cup in the sun gets warmer, which makes the water evaporate faster. The cup in the cupboard is cooler and darker, so evaporation happens much more slowly. This shows that heat from the sun is an important part of the water cycle - it helps water evaporate from oceans, lakes, and rivers.

ideas.title: Boiling Water Cloud Chamber
steps: Place the small cup in the center of the large bowl
Have your teacher pour boiling water into the large bowl around the small cup (not in the small cup)
Quickly place the metal pan on top of the bowl like a lid
Put ice cubes on top of the metal pan
Watch carefully what happens under the pan
You will see a cloud form inside the bowl
Watch as water droplets form on the bottom of the pan
See the droplets drip down into the small cup like rain
This creates a mini water cycle you can watch in action
ideas.why_it_works: The hot water evaporates quickly and rises as water vapor. When this vapor touches the cold pan (cooled by ice), it condenses back into tiny water droplets, forming a cloud. As more droplets join together, they become heavy and fall as 'rain' into the cup. This models how clouds form in the sky when warm, moist air rises and cools, and how rain falls when water droplets combine and get too heavy to float.
